coredata.dc:title: Green florescent carbon dots synthesized from various household green wastes for detection of parathion methyl pesticide
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Medhi, Manisha
Yumnam, Monica
Mudoi, Pronab
Mishra, Poonam
coredata.dc:description: The purpose of the current study was to examine the use of green household wastes, such as lemon peel, bottle gourd peel (BG), culinary banana peel, and sugarcane bagasse, as a source for synthesizing carbon dots (C-dots) and to determine how the composition of the material affected the functional characteristics of the C-dots and its interaction behaviour for detection of pesticide. The synthesized C-dots showed green fluorescence with varying particle sizes and red shifting fluorescence with more than 100 nm Stokes shift, indicating time and excitation dependent luminescence behaviour. C-dots produced from bottle gourd (BG) peel had a positive impact on the functional characteristics with a maximum emission of 514 nm and excitation of 410 nm. The SAED and TEM pattern confirmed its amorphous structure. The hydronium ion diameter and the quantum yield were 0.99 nm and 3.1 % respectively. Contributing towards food safety, the synthesized C-dots from BG showed significant behaviour in the detection of parathion methyl (MP) like organophosphorus pesticide (OP) as it conjugated with iron and 1 ppm of parathion methyl, which exhibited “Turn-Off-On” fluorescence behaviour with noticeable changed in intensity and act as a promising qualitative tool for detection of parathion methyl in real sample. The intensity of FL and concentration of OP is directly proportional which is established by the investigated OP in vegetable, fruit and water samples. The significance of iron and MP-OP was also investigated in the presence of other metal ions and pesticides and showed that the C-dots synthesized probe solution was reliable and sensitive towards the detection of MP-OP in real samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Food waste
Fluorescence
Quantum yield
Organophosphate pesticide

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Methodology of synthesis of luminescence C dots
Preparation of probe solution to detection of parathion methyl (PM-OP)
C-dots probe solution and PM-OP solution
Calibration, limit of detection (LOD), limit of quantification (LOQ) and validation of MP-OP
Selectivity of parathion methyl in presence of other pesticide
Selectivity of the C-dots probe towards other metal ion
Detection of parathion methyl in real sample: vegetable, fruit, water
Proximate, CHN analysis, yield, and concentration determination
Fluorescence of C-dot probe solution at different pH
Effect of metal ions in the C-dot probe solution
Effect of interaction of pesticide and C-dots on the optical and chemical behavior of C-dots
Selectivity, calibration, LOD, and LOQ of MP-OP
Turn-on’ fluorescence of C-dots in real sample analysis

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Concentration and yield of synthesized C-dots from the peel powder of culinary banana, sugar cane bagasse, bottle gourd and lemon,(b) C-dots under i. normal light, ii. UV light-from left-culinary banana, sugar cane bagasse, bottle gourd, lemon, and (c) UV–Vis spectra of synthesized C-dots.

(a)FTIR of synthesized C-dots- KK-culinary banana peel; SC-sugarcane bagasse; BG-bottle gourd peel; L-lemon peel; (b–e) Fluorescence spectra of synthesized C-dots, (b) Culinary banana peel; (c) Sugarcane bagasse; (d) Bottle gourd peel; and (e) Lemon peel.

(a)–(d)_Particle size distribution of synthesized C-dots- (a) culinary banana peel; (b) sugarcane bagasse; (c) bottle gourd peel; and (d) lemon peel, (e)–(h) TEM micrograpgh of C-dots from Bottle gourd peel- (e) at 20 nm with d-spacing, inset); (f) at 5 nm; (g) size distribution; (h) SAED pattern of C-dots.
Raman Spectra of synthesized C-dots- (a) culinary banana peel; (b) sugarcane bagasse; (c) bottle gourd peel; and (d) lemon peel.
FL Spectra of C-dots probe- (a) at different pH; (b) with different metal ion.

(a) UV–VIS spectra of C-dots of BG with 1 ppm parathion methyl pesticide, (b) UV–Vis spectra of parathion methyl pesticide, (c) calibration curve of C-dots of BG with parathion methyl pesticide, (d) FTIR spectra of C-dots- BG and C-dots of BG with 1 ppm parathion methyl pesticide, (e) C-dots, C-dots probe solution with 1 ppm parathion methyl pesticide, C-dots probe solution under normal and UV light.

FL spectra of C-dotsprobe of BG with various concentration of (a) parathion methyl pesticide (MP), (b) glyphosate pesticide (Gly), (c) atraxine pesticide (ATR), (d) Deltamethrinpesticide (Del), (e) DDT pesticide (DDT), (f) Chlorpyrifos pesticide (Chl).

FL spectra of synthesized C-dots- BG, C-dots probe solution and C-dots probe of BG with 1 ppm pesticides of (a) parathion methyl (MP), (b) glyphosate (Gly), (c) atraxine(ATR), (d) Deltamethrin (Del), (e) DDT (DDT), (f) Chlorpyrifos (Chl).
(a) Extraction of cabbage and apple after spike with OP; FL Spectra at different concentration of OP, (b) apple, (c) Cabbage, (d) Well water.
Proximate and CHN analysis of raw powdered and synthesized C-dots.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Note: Value in row with different superscript differ significantly.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Found values and percent recovery at various concentration of MP-OP + C-dots solution (ppm).

row.entry: Concentration of Methyl parathion (ppm)in C dots probe solution
Observed values from C dots probe solution

head.ce:title: Green florescent carbon dots synthesized from various household green wastes for detection of parathion methyl pesticide

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Food Engineering and Technology, Tezpur University, Tezpur, 784028, Assam, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Food Engineering and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Food Engineering and Technology, Tezpur University, Tezpur- 784028, Assam, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Molecular Biology and Biotechnology, Tezpur University, Tezpur, 784028, Assam, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Molecular Biology and Biotechnology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Molecular Biology and Biotechnology, Tezpur University, Tezpur- 784028, Assam, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The purpose of the current study was to examine the use of green household wastes, such as lemon peel, bottle gourd peel (BG), culinary banana peel, and sugarcane bagasse, as a source for synthesizing carbon dots (C-dots) and to determine how the composition of the material affected the functional characteristics of the C-dots and its interaction behaviour for detection of pesticide. The synthesized C-dots showed green fluorescence with varying particle sizes and red shifting fluorescence with more than 100 nm Stokes shift, indicating time and excitation dependent luminescence behaviour. C-dots produced from bottle gourd (BG) peel had a positive impact on the functional characteristics with a maximum emission of 514 nm and excitation of 410 nm. The SAED and TEM pattern confirmed its amorphous structure. The hydronium ion diameter and the quantum yield were 0.99 nm and 3.1 % respectively. Contributing towards food safety, the synthesized C-dots from BG showed significant behaviour in the detection of parathion methyl (MP) like organophosphorus pesticide (OP) as it conjugated with iron and 1 ppm of parathion methyl, which exhibited “Turn-Off-On” fluorescence behaviour with noticeable changed in intensity and act as a promising qualitative tool for detection of parathion methyl in real sample. The intensity of FL and concentration of OP is directly proportional which is established by the investigated OP in vegetable, fruit and water samples. The significance of iron and MP-OP was also investigated in the presence of other metal ions and pesticides and showed that the C-dots synthesized probe solution was reliable and sensitive towards the detection of MP-OP in real samples.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (C-dots), the carrier of dazzling fluorescent qualities have a size of less than 10 nm and are also known as quasi-spherical carbonaceous nano material, with sp2 carbon and can contain oxygen, nitrogen, hydrogen, phosphorus, sulphur, and boron which improve the fascinating fluorescent properties [1]. The fluorescent C-dots are vary in colour from blue, green and red as extending towards a longer wavelength [2].

C-dots have been synthesized using a variety of sources to date. One of the key reasons for the growing interest in C-dots is the large number of opening sources for synthesizing this fascinating structure [1]. Natural materials are becoming a new platform for the creation of highly intriguing C-dots. The different precursors reported to date to synthesize C-dots with various size, are folic acid [3], aspartic acid [4], m-aminobenzoic acid [5], citric acid [6], waste polyolefins [7], caffeine [8], papaya powder [9], Lycii Fructus [10], bael leaves (Aegle marmelos) powder [11], tulshi leaves (Ocimum sanctum) [12] etc. The synthesized C-dots till date reported promising utilization in the field of bioimaging, pH measurement, spoilage detection, detection of heavy metals and pesticides, etc. [2].

The two main categories of the C-dots synthesis route are top-down and bottom-up approaches, each of which has subcategories of various techniques [13]. Among bottom-up methods, pyrolysis [8], hydrothermal [12,13], microwave-assisted methods [4], are widely used because they are simple, cost-effective and less time-consuming. With the advancement of research, these synthesis routes are gaining favour as they produce C-dots having different sizes and properties.

The hydrothermal and microwave-assisted methods are widely reported methods as they are low cost and easy to handle. The microwave-assisted one significantly reduced the fabrication time with improved yields and purities of the product and gives efficient and homogeneous heating in comparison to other hours taking methodologies [4]. Microwave-assisted carbonization approach is also used to produce graphene quantum dots (GQDs) with various carbohydrates as an opening material.

As the population increases and society progresses toward industrialization, food processing waste is becoming a serious concern. It could be kitchen waste, waste generated during industrial or domestic processing, waste of peels, seeds, pomace and so on. According to FAO (Food and Agriculture Organization of the United Nations) research, in 2013, food waste accounts (around 1.6 million tons per year) for one-third of the total food produced for human consumption. Food wastes are mostly made up of organic components and therefore can be used to synthesize C-dots. Also, the increase in waste material utilizing leads to reducing the costs associated with their disintegration. Waste materials are increasingly being used as precursor materials in many research investigations since they are a useful source of a variety of beneficial components. Being a rich source of carbon, it can be effectively utilized for the synthesis of C-dots. With the rise in popularity of green chemistry, researchers are increasingly turning the low-cost, renewable sources such as fruit juices and green wastes such as watermelon peels, pineapple peels, cucumber peels, and so on to synthesize C-dots [14].

Pesticides are the chemical constituent widely used to increase productivity by protecting the different cultivation from pests, to reduce post-harvest losses, and to eliminate the hunger crisis. Most of the time in vegetables like for cultivation of cabbage, pesticides are used in the agriculture field. Water is being polluted as the pesticide residues used in the agriculture field go towards the nearby water area and unfortunately in many areas that water is one of the sources of drinking water. Organophosphorus pesticides (OPs) are most widely used in agriculture due to their high efficiency in insect elimination [15]. However, the concern is the actual amount to be used for the same, as a small amount of OPs can produce acute or chronic neurotoxicity by inhibiting the enzyme acetylcholinesterase (AChE), which regulates nerve impulses [16]. For the detection of organophosphorus pesticides (OPs), enzyme-based sensing is widely used, where due to the enzyme's specificity being attractive towards recognition elements in those biosensors for the target pesticides. The most commonly used enzyme for the detection of pesticides is Acetylcholinesterase (AChE) which is found in the nervous system. As OPs interact with the AChE, accumulation of acetylcholine (ACh) occurs which induces overstimulation of the peripheral as well as the central cholinergic nervous systems [17]. Hence accurate use of the OPs and detection of the same is of utmost importance and concern. Detection of the same is generally done through HPLC, GCMS, and spectroscopic techniques which are costly and time-consuming. Henceforth, the alternate concept for the same as to develop novel and cost-effective next-generation sensor systems is gaining importance.

Different biosensors woks as probe which are generally consist of a signal transducer like C-dots, QDs which are linked with biological recognition element like enzyme or cell or antibody [18]. QD based biosensor had been widely explored for the detection OPs based on AChE. An optically based sensor provides a facile, rapid, and low-cost way for the detection of pesticides based on fluorescence (FL). Generally, it works as the recognition point interacts with the target analyte, like pesticides, and a transducer point for signalling the binding event [19]. The signalling can be obtained in “turn-on-off-on” mode. Based on the recognition point, different strategies of signalling can be classified as enzyme-based, aptamer-based, antibody-based etc [20]. Different pesticides like paraxon and chlorpyrifos were detected by C-dots’ FL behaviour, which were synthesized from different kinds of natural and chemical precursors [21]. Yan et al. in their study developed a probe for detection of parathion methyl pesticide based on the use of CdTe QDs, where they reported that the presence of the pesticide quenched the PL intensity of the QDs [22]. Paraxon pesticide was reported to be detected by the utilization of egg white encapsulated gold nanoclusters [20].

Many foods itself contain C-dots and various studies had been done focusing on the inborne toxicity of the same. During heating process the precursor in food developed the nano structures. Heating and processing aid of food may develop many nano structured materials and the universal presence of C-dots in food items has raised questions about the formation process and encourage researchers to investigate the mechanism and issue regarding their safety alarms and probable bio-effects [23].

There are insufficient statistics is available on food-borne C-dots bio-distribution, and more attention is focused on the fate of food-borne C-dots after oral exposure. Jiang et al., investigated in vitro bio-distribution of coffee C-dots in an SMMC-7721 cell line, and the results revealed that those cells could uptake C-dots [24]. There is need of standard evaluation method to evaluate of C-dots health risks. However, in our study we developed C-dots from food wastes which are not generally consumed. Also, the developed C-dots is used as probe for detection. Hence, there less possibility towards toxicity effect in our present study.

Limited comparative characterizations are available on C-dots produced from everyday produce kitchen wastes. The reported work was carried out in the two subheads, where a comparative study on the characterization of functional properties of C-dots from the peel of bottle gourd (BG), Assam lemon, culinary banana, and sugar cane bagasse is carried out. The outcome of the study will be helpful for an appropriate selection of raw material for the synthesis of functional C-dots without using any doping techniques. Further C-dots probe solution was developed and interaction behaviour between C-dots with parathion methyl was studied to develop a consumer-friendly sensing technique for detection of parathion methyl pesticide in real food samples. The selectivity of the synthesized C-dots for parathion methyl was also investigated in the presence of other pesticides and metal ions.

Lemon (Citrus limon), bottle gourd (Lagenaria siceraria), and culinary banana (Musa ABB) (Kachkal) were collected from Horticulture Department of Tezpur University, Tezpur; however, sugarcane (Saccharum officinarum) bagasse was procured from Tezpur Town, Assam, India. To maintain the uniformity, all samples collected from a single lot and were dried and converted into powder and used for the synthesis of C-dots. The cabbage, apple was procured from Tezpur Town, Assam and water as a real sample was collected from nearby areas of Tezpur University. Quinine sulphate, (NH4)2Fe(SO4)2.6H2O, acetylcholine esterase (AChE), acetylcholine (ACh), aholine oxidase (ChOx), parathion methyl, glyphosate, atrazine, DDT, deltamethrin, chlorpyrifos, metal ion solution of Na, K, Mn, Fe, Cu, Pb was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich, Germany and the other analytical grade chemicals used for analysis were procured from Sigma-Aldrich, Germany and HiMedia, Mumbai, India.

Collected peels and bagasse were individually sorted, graded, and cleaned thoroughly with tap water followed by double distilled water to remove the visible contaminants. The samples were dried in a tray dryer (IKON Instruments, Delhi) at 45 °C for 15–18 h and further converted into powder using Lab Grain Mill (LaboTech, Germany) with a 5 mm sieve. The powdered samples were collected and stored in an airtight container at 5 °C for further analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Methodology of synthesis of luminescence C dots
ce:section.ce:para: The powdered samples (0.70 g) of lemon (Citrus limon), bottle gourd (Lagenaria siceraria), culinary banana (Musa ABB) (Kachkal) and sugarcane (Saccharum officinarum) bagasse were taken in 1000 mL beaker separately and thoroughly mixed in 100 mL of double distilled water and carbonized using microwave reactor (LG-IFB 20) [25–27] at 900 W for 17 min. After 17 min of treatment, the increase in time caused the burning of the sample instead of the carbonization except in sugarcane bagasse. Thus, 17 min opted for the carbonization process and were kept constant for all the samples to maintain the uniformity in the independent variable. The brown colour residues obtained after microwave treatment were cooled up to room temperature and then 50 mL of double distilled water was added. The mixture was centrifuged at 7000 rpm for 20 min at 4 °C (5430R, Eppendorf, Hamburg) to eliminate the larger particles. The collected supernatants were filtered using a 0.22 μm nitrocellulose membrane (HiMedia) and packed in airtight container and were stored in dark and refrigerated conditions for further analysis [28].

Moisture content and ash content of the powdered sample were analysed according to the AOAC (21st ed.) [29]. However, carbohydrates, crude fat, crude protein, and crude fibre were estimated by as per the method given by Sadasivam and Manickam [30].

CHN analysis was done by CHN analyzer (PerkinElmer, USA, Model-2400 SERIES-2) for both powdered raw samples and synthesized C-dots samples to determine the percentage of carbon, hydrogen, and nitrogen in the samples.

Optical characteristics of C-dots were studied by UV–Visible spectrophotometer (Cary 60 UV–VIS spectrophotometer, Agilent UNICO Products and Instruments Inc., Shanghai, China, software- Cary Win). UV Vis spectra were taken by scanning the samples from 200 to 800 nm [33].

Photoluminescence and fluorescence intensity were measured by a Fluorescence spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer LS5S, software: FL Win Lab). Photoluminescence properties were measured by analysing the emission spectra of C-dots at different excitation wavelengths ranging from 310 to 510 nm wavelengths with continuous spans of 20 nm [31].

Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy (Impact 410 Nicolet FTIR spectrometer, USA, software- OMNIC E.S.P.5.0) was performed in the spectral window from 400 to 4000 cm−1 to study the different bond structure of functional group [32].

To determine the hydronium ion diameter of the synthesized C-dots, Dynamic Light Scattering (DLS) (Microtrac Wave, software- Microtrac FLEX 11.0.0.3) test was performed. In brief, the first sample was mixed with known quantities of water and ultrasonicated for 10 min, a few drops of the sample were placed on the analysis plate and the diameter was recorded [33].

TEM (Transmission Electron Microscopy) study was performed at different wavelengths to reveal the size and morphology of C-dots (FEI Company, USA Model: TECNAI G2 20 S-Twin (200 KV); Resolution: 2.4A0). Particle size distribution was done with Image J software.

Raman spectra (EZ RAMAN-N) of synthesized C-dots were recorded using laser beam of 785 nm in dark and ambient temperature to know the structural formations. By Raman spectroscopy, the D band and G band were analysed, and ID/IG ratio was calculated as per Equation (1) to know the degree of graphitization [34].
(1)
I D
I G
=
(
A r e a
u n d e r
c u r v e
o f
D
b a n d
)
(
A r e a
u n d e r
c u r v e
o f
G
b a n d
)

The Quantum yields of C-dots were estimated at 360 nm using quinine sulphate as standard (0.1 M H₂SO₄ solution) (QY: 0.54) (QR). Absorption and emission peak were recorded at 360 nm [31]. QY of the C-dots was calculated using Equation (2).
(2)
Q =
Q R
×
I
I R
×
A R
A
×
η 2
η R 2
Where Q is the QY, I is the measured integrated fluorescent emission intensity, η is the refractive index of the solvent, and A refers to the absorbance. The subscript R refers to the corresponding parameter of the known fluorescent standard. The η for both sample and standard are 1 [31].

The synthesized C-dots solution was dried at 40 °C for 24 h in a hot air oven (Eyela, NDO 700 W) and the yield percentage (%) was calculated as per the formula given in Equation 3
(3)
Y i e l d
( % )
=
W e i g h t
o f
s a m p l e s
a f t e r
d r y i n g
( g )
I n i t i a l
w e i g h t
o f
s a m p l e
t a k e n
( g )
× 100

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of probe solution to detection of parathion methyl (PM-OP)

ce:section.ce:para: For preparation of C-dots probe solution, in 40 mL of C-dots solution (source bottle gourd peel)(3 mg/mL), 0.0294 g (NH4)2(FeSO4)26H2O was solubilized and diluted to 50 mL with citric acid and disodium hydrogen phosphate buffer (0.2 M, pH = 4). Digital pH meter was used to check the pH of the probe solution and FL spectra of the same at different pH was recorded to check the effect of pH on fluorescence behaviour of the probe solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: C-dots probe solution and PM-OP solution
ce:section.ce:para: For detection of parathion methyl, the C-dot probe solution as mentioned in 2.5.1 was prepared and checked the interaction with the three pesticides [21]. For detection of parathion methyl pesticide, 1 mL of AChE (2.2 U/mL) was mixed with 5 mL of parathion methyl (1 ppm) in a test tube and placed in an incubator at 37 °C for 10 min. After that 1 mL of ACh(0.01 M) and 5 mL of ChOx (0.1 U/mL) were added and again placed in an incubator at 37 °C for 20 min. The 1 mL of developed solution was taken out in a test tube and mixed with 9 mL of C-dots probe solution and kept in dark for further analysis [21]. The sample mixture was then analysed for UV–VIS spectroscopy, fluorescence spectroscopy and FTIR Spectroscopy to study the interaction and conjugation behaviour of pesticides with the respective C-dots solution. The MP-OP alone was also studied for UV–VIS spectroscopy to check the conjugation by scanning the sample from 200 to 800 nm [31].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Calibration, limit of detection (LOD), limit of quantification (LOQ) and validation of MP-OP
ce:section.ce:para:
A calibration curve was established between the different concentrations (0.02–0.1 ppm) of MP-OP + C-dots and fluorescence intensity and the linear equation obtained was used for quantitative analysis of the sample. The LOD and LOQ were determined by fluorescence intensity of MP-OP + C-dots at the lowest concentration and calculated using Equation (4) and Equation (5)
.
(4)
LOD =
3
σ
S
(5)
LOQ =
10
σ
S
Where σ represents the standard deviation of the response of the blank sample, and S represents the slope of the calibration curve obtained from the fluorescence intensity of different concentrations of MP-OP.

For validation, the presence of MP-OP was determined by Gas Chromatography 7890A GC system (Agilent Technologies Palo Alto, CA, USA) that was equipped with J & W DB-1mS fused silica capillary column (30m × 0.32 mm × 0.1μ) with N2 and Ar as carrier gas. The MP-OP solution was diluted in hexane (1:1), and 1 μL sample was injected at slit mode (5:1) manually with an injection temperature of 250 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of parathion methyl in presence of other pesticide
ce:section.ce:para: To check the selectivity of synthesized C-dots towards parathion methyl, investigation was carried out with other five different pesticides namely glyphosate (Gly) and chlorpyrifos (Chl) which are an organophosphate and atrazine (ATR), DDT and deltamethrin (Del). The preparation of samples for analysis was as per described in section 2.5 where PM-OP was replaced by the other five pesticides separately.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of the C-dots probe towards other metal ion
ce:section.ce:para: For selectivity study of the C-dots toward different metal ions, various stock solution (10 ppm) of metal ion solution of Na+, K+, Mn2+, Fe3+, Cu2+, Pb2+were prepared by using Millipore water. Then 1 mL of Fe3+ (1 ppm) solution was added into the C-dots probe solution. The change in FL behaviour was compared with C-dots solution of BG and C-dot probe solution by taking the FL spectra at 410 nm excitation after 10 min of reaction time. The same procedure was used for other metals also [35].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of parathion methyl in real sample: vegetable, fruit, water
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the real sample application by this detection method, the investigation was carried out in samples, i.e., cabbage, apple and well water sample. Parathion methyl was added in the concentration of 1 ppm and 10 ppm in cabbage and apple and in 0.5 ppm and 5 ppm in water sample.

Cabbage and apple were washed properly with clean water followed by distilled water to remove the dust followed by dipping in distilled water for 20 min and wiping thoroughly with tissue paper. Samples were cut into small pieces, 20 g of each sample was taken in three parts (blank, 1 ppm, 10 ppm). 1 mL of parathion methyl solution of 1 ppm and 10 ppm concentration were sprayed over the sample and waited for 1 h for proper absorption. Then 40 mL of phosphate buffer solution (1.0 M, pH-7.4) solution was added to the samples and ground with a mixer grinder. The ground sample solutions were centrifuged (3000 rpm, 20 min, 10 °C) and the collected supernatant was filtered using Whatman No. 1 filter paper. The filtrate was collected in a clean airtight sample bottle and stored at 4 °C for further analysis.

In the water sample, the concentration of parathion methyl used was 0.5 ppm and 5 ppm. 1 mL (1 ppm) parathion methyl pesticide was added to 20 mL of the water sample and kept for 1 h proper interaction.

A series of tubes were arranged for the three real sample additions to the blank. In each test tube 1 mL of AChE (2.2 U/mL) was mixed with 5 mL of sample solutions, mixed using a vortex and placed in an incubator at 37 °C for 10 min. After that 1 mL of 0.01 M ACh and 5 mL of ChOx (0.1 U/mL) were added and placed water bath for 20 min (37 °C). After that, 1 mL of the above solution was taken out in a test tube and mixed with 9 mL of C-dots probe solution and kept in the dark for 1 h [21]. Then sample mixture was run in the Fluorescence Spectroscopy at 410 excitation wavelength to study the interaction of pesticides.

The data from the device was transferred to an excel file using the freeware software Parallax Acquisition for Microcontrollers (PLX DAQ). Armonk, NY: IBM SPSS Statistics Version 20.0 was adopted for statistical analyze the sample. Origin Pro 2018 was used to create the graphs (Origin Lab Corporation).

Formation of C-dots is a plausible mechanism of carbonization and is a complicated one and confined to pyrolysis of the source or the sample, hybridization, and electron transfer of sp3 to sp2-hybridized carbon and reorganisation of bond and structure [36]. The process of carbonization involves various chemical reactions with the dissociation of older and the formation of newer bond structures [31]. From the above-mentioned raw materials, the C-dots were fortuitously produced via microwave assisted synthetic route. The four samples used in the present investigation were a rich source of simple to complex carbohydrates and CHN analysis also showed that they were a good source of carbon (Table 1 ), which assisted the structural formation of C-dots [37]. In a controlled environment of synthesis of C-dots, dehydration of carbohydrates occurred as the reactions proceeded and formed compounds like furfural and hydroxymethyl furfural [38]. These formed components undergo aldol and self-condensation, followed by polymerization by forming polymers of the structure like the carbon cluster via interaction with covalent, non-covalent and other means. Following the interactions aromatization took place with the formation of aromatic ring structures and the emergence of double bonded conjugated structure and finally carbonization occurred and beyond saturation, a nuclear burst took place with the evolution of water-soluble fluorescence C-dots. The colour exhibited during the fluorescence is governed by the size of the C-dots. With the increase in size, the colour of the solution changes and tends to longer wavelength [37].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Proximate, CHN analysis, yield, and concentration determination
ce:section.ce:para: All the analysed samples were rich in carbohydrates followed by the crude fibre (Table 1). This signified that the analysed samples were rich sources of carbon and positively affected the idea of using these samples as a green precursor for the synthesis of C-dots (Table 1). The same may be correlated with the FTIR also, where the intensity of –OH peak was found to be highest. Other inorganic materials like K, Na, Fe, Zn, S, P, and Br contribute to the percentage of composition as the peels are rich in different inorganic components. C-dots synthesized from BG only showed a detectable amount of nitrogen (0.12 %), however, the other three samples may have very low levels of nitrogen which could not be detected by the method used for analysis. The yield and concentration found for C-dots synthesized from the peel of culinary banana, bagasse of sugarcane, peel of BG and lemon were 0.23 %, 0.12 %, 0.47 %, 0.36 % and 2.20 g/L, 1.16 g/L, 4.58 g/L, 3.48 g/L, respectively (Fig. 1 a). C-dots synthesized from BG peel had comparatively more yield and concentration than the C-dots synthesized from other sources.

The C-dots solution showed brown colour in normal light and green colour under excitation in UV light as shown in Fig. 1b- (i) and (ii) respectively. The UV–Vis absorption peaks for the peel of culinary banana, bagasse of sugarcane, peel of BG and lemon were found at 281 nm, 282 nm, 283 nm and 285 nm respectively (Fig. 1c) with a tail extension towards the visible region suggesting the formation of C=C with π-π∗ transition and n–π∗ transition of the C=O bond. De and Karak (2013) also reported the UV–Vis absorption at 283 nm. The UV–Vis absorption peak of synthesized C-dots were at around 250 nm–300 nm suggested the absorption for the aromatic π system, which was like polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons of C-dots [28].

PL behavior of C-dots is a most fascinating characteristic along with topping with a good stoke shift increases the value of it in practical applications. A continuous scan was done for all samples with different excitation wavelengths from 310 nm to 490 nm with a span of 20 nm to find the maximum emission at a particular wavelength. The red shifting of the emission wavelength was seen with an increase in the excitation wavelength, but during the continuous scans, the intensities of the emission spectra first increased, and after reaching a particular excitation wavelength, the intensities of the emission wavelength decreased noticeably. With excitation wavelengths of 350 nm, 390 nm, 410 nm, and 350 nm for the samples of peel of culinary banana, bagasse of sugarcane, peel of BG and lemon, the maximum emission peaks were found at wavelengths of 454 nm, 493 nm, 514 nm, and 485 nm with stoke shifts of 104 nm, 103 nm, 104 nm, 135 nm respectively (Fig. 2 b, c, 2d, 2e). The fluorescence intensity of the samples showed that both emission and intensity vary with the difference in excitation wavelength, which can be defined with the term excitation-dependent fluorescence emitting [38]. The mechanism and chemistry behind this phenomenon can be explained by the carbon cores non uniformity in size, a different functional group in the surface sites of the C-dots as a different range of surface traps, pH of the C-dots solution and concentration of the synthesized C-dots solution [39]. The difference in size and carbon content of the core favours the emission of different intensities of fluorescence with different excitations of its photon. As a function of that, the word arises in the C-dot chapter, the “excitation-dependent fluorescence emitting C-dots”. Also, the nature of the surface traps, and their bonding have an affect on the FL behaviour of the C-dots. So, the dependent variable for the whole PL mechanism is the size and surface of the C-dots together with the precursor, the solvent used, the reaction variables, defect sites, zig-zag sites in the surface, quantum confinement and pH of the solution [39]. Here in the tested samples also, size and the composition along with pH affected the FL behaviour, which can be correlated with the result of nitrogen percentage and dietary fibre percentage along with the reported particle size (Fig. 3 a–d). With an increase in pH value, the emission peak intensities decreased.

The QY of C-dots from precursor culinary materials: banana peel, sugarcane bagasse, BG and lemon were 0.33, 0.35, 3.1 and 0.81 % respectively. The low QY was due to the approach of synthesizing the sample in a completely greener technological way. The use of the natural medium of reaction as a solvent, i.e. water, and no modifications like passivation and doping along with pH may be the most influencing reasons for the lower quantum yield. The existence of functional groups like nitrogen enhances the quantum yield as N trapped electrons with radiative recombination suppress the non-radiative recombination [36].

Comparing the constitutional percentage of all four raw powdered samples, it was observed that in culinary banana powder, the ash percentage was on the higher side indicating the presence of impurities, which might be the reason for ceasing the FL behaviour of the synthesized C-dots. Also, the sample had the lowest value of yield and concentration percentage, which supported the lower intensities of UV–VIS peak, lowest emissionmaxFL, and lower QY compared with the other three samples. Due to their identical constitutional percentages of C and H in the starting raw powdered sample, the C-dots sample solution of culinary banana peel and lemon peel had the same excitation wavelength in which both samples had emissionmax FL value. Lemon peel powder carried higher percentage of nitrogen than the culinary banana peel powder, which may be influenced the fluorescence and gave the higher value of emissionmax despite the same excitation wavelength. The N percent played a significant role in the investigation as the raw BG contained a higher percentage of N and C-dots of BG only contained detectable amount of N (Table 1). This favoured the highest value and intensity of UV–VIS peak, emissionmax FL, and QY percent in C-dots of BG. The finding was supported by a previous study as nitrogen played a great role in enhancing the photoluminescence behaviour and many times used as a doping element during the synthesis of C-dots [8,40].

The absence of nitrogen in sugarcane bagasse and the presence of a higher percentage of fibre reduced the fluorescence property of the C-dots synthesized from sugarcane bagasse. As the polysaccharide percentage was higher in the sample, it might be difficult to dissociate and form a new bond in the experimental condition and as a result, somehow, might be the whole sample was not utilized to establish the aromatization as well as the carbonizationin17 min, emitting lower fluorescence and larger particles size.

The concentration of the C-dots is relatable with the concentration of carbon in the sample and the distance or proximity between and among the C-dots in the solution. This influences the peak and intensity of absorbance, excitation, and emission behaviour. With increasing the concentration of the C-dots solution, the fluorescence behaviour increases [41]. In present finding, it was seen that in the BG sample, the fluorescence behaviour was more pronounced with a higher emission wavelength, as the concentration and yield of the same were at the highest compared to the other tested samples.

The FTIR analysis showed peaks in the range of 3443–3449 cm−1 for the O-H vibration and in 1630–1637 cm−1 for the >C=C< bonding (Fig. 2a). The spectrum showed a peak in the region of 2914–2921 cm−1 which was profound due to the formation of bond –CH3, >CH2, -C-H and indicated the presence of aliphatic hydrocarbons. C=C stretching was observed at 1494 cm−1 in the BG C-dots sample. The different functional groups with the linkage of oxygen suggested that the synthesized C-dots samples were well dispersed in water. Also, the formation of the double bond in carbon and the presence of an alkyl group indicated the formation of C-dots.

To form mono dispersed homogeneous C-dots via microwave assisted method is somehow difficult as an aggregation of the nano dots is an unavoidable circumstance in this process due to the method's as well as the instrument's tendency to completely dry the sugar liquid. Due to this kind of aggregation, the sizes of the C-dots formed, vary. Fig. 3a-d showed that the hydronium ion diameter of the synthesized C-dots from sample peel of culinary banana, bagasse of sugarcane, peel of BG peel, and lemon, had size distribution of 1.06 nm, 102.6 nm, 0.99 nm, and 458 nm respectively. The C-dots synthesized from the BG showed a very small size supporting the formation of C-dots. But in lemon powder, the crude fibre percentage was high and the synthesized C-dots solution had higher value of C and H %, which favoured the aggregation of the C-dots after synthesis with time in respect to the other three samples. Hence the obtained particle size was found on a much higher side. In the presence of a higher percentage of dietary fiber in sugarcane bagasse and lemon peel, proper carbonization did not occur Also, the size differences might be correlated with the percentage of dietary fibre and polysaccharide present, as it was seen that sugarcane bagasse and lemon peel contained higher percentages of the same, which obstruct the proper breakage and reformation of the bond to form the required carbonization for the formation of C-dots. These affected the fluorescence behaviour of synthesized C-dots as it was noticed that both C-dots samples of sugarcane bagasse and lemon peel showed a similar range of emissionmax. Based on the particle size and other properties, BG C-dots was selected for further study.

The average size of respective C-dots was found in the range of 1–1.2 nm which was close to the data obtained from the DLS study (Fig. 3c). The C-dots were spherical and evenly distributed in the plane. The d-spacing of the same was 0.21 nm (Fig. 3e, inset) which is nearly in tune with the graphitic carbon [40]. The poor crystalline form of C-dots was seen in Selected Area Electron Diffraction (SAED) analysis, which may be due to the presence of oxygen group on the surfaces of C-dots. The SAED pattern also revealed that the synthesized C-dots were of amorphous in structure, and were properly synthesized [42].

The Raman spectra as shown in Fig. 4 , gave the idea of the formation of graphite like structures. The characteristics peaks for D and G bands were found at 1436 cm−1 and 1532 cm−1, 1466 cm−1 and 1552 cm−1, 1454 cm−1 and 1586 cm−1, 1446 cm−1 and 1526 cm−1 for C-dots of culinary banana peel, sugarcane bagasse, BG peel and lemon peel, respectively. The ID/IG ratio for the samples was found 0.94, 0.93, 0.90, and 0.94 for the C-dots samples synthesized from the culinary banana peel, sugarcane bagasse, BG peel and lemon peel, respectively. The D band speaks about the hexagonal lattice disorder and defects, and the G band arises due to the vibration of sp2 carbon atoms of the 2D hexagonal lattice. The calculated ID/IG ratio indicates the atomic ratio of sp2/sp3 carbons [43], which can be used for correlation of structural properties with graphite-like structure [44].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence of C-dot probe solution at different pH
ce:section.ce:para: The original pH of the C-dot probe solution was found 2.71. The solutions pH from 2.71 to 11.0 was changed and studied the effect of pH on fluorescence intensity of C-dots probe solution at 410 nm. Fluorescence intensity decreased gradually with the increased in pH value of the solution (pH 2.71 to 11.0) (Fig. 5 a). Maximum fluorescence intensity was observed in pH 2.71, therefore, C-dots probe of having 2.71 pH was used in further study [35].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of metal ions in the C-dot probe solution
ce:section.ce:para: For the selectivity of Iron in ‘turn off’ mechanism, the fluorescence intensities of the C-dots probe solution was recorded at 410 nm excitation in the presence and absence of iron ions. Fe3+(1 ppm) was found to be having ability of quenching the PL intensity of the C-dots probe solution with a sharp decrease in the intensity while other metal ions (Na+, K+, Mn2+, Cu2+, Pb2+) showed a little effects compared to Fe3+ (Fig. 5b). This supported that other metal ions showed no interference toward the ‘turn off’ mechanism of Fe3+in developed probe solution [10].

As in the C-dots surface C-OH/C-O-C groups were present, the hydroxyl groups on these C-dots could interact with Fe3+ and form complexes which may trigger up fluorescence quenching due to the nonradiative electron/hole recombination through effective photoelectron or energy transfer. The other possible reason of the C-dots's excellent selectivity toward Fe3+ sensing might be due to the coordination of hydroxyl groups with Fe3+ and the unfilled orbit of Fe3+ [10].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of interaction of pesticide and C-dots on the optical and chemical behavior of C-dots
ce:section.ce:para: UV–VIS spectra of BG showed the characteristics of the aromatic pi system through the formation of C=C with π-π∗ transition and n–π∗ transition of the C=O bond contributing peak at 283 nm (Fig. 1c) responsible for fluorescence behaviour under UV light (Fig. 1b–ii). But the solution of BG + Pesticide which was an interactive mixture solution of C-dots Probe solution and OP, showed a higher and some other noisy peak around 236 nm, 270 nm, 305 nm, and 363 nm (Fig. 6 a) due to the formation of more amide bonds and conjugation [45], which were found to be absent in MP-OP solution (Fig. 6b) suggesting the conjugation of MP-OP with the C-dot probe solution. The parathion methyl often deforms into p-nitrophenol and dimethyl thiophosphonate when it dissociated during the interaction with reactive components [46]. Previous studies showed parathion methyl formed the distinctive peaks at 236 nm and 270 nm which affirm our findings [47,48]. Addition to that, the red shift of the wavelength towards 400 nm indicated interaction-conjugation of the reaction material and synthesis of p-nitrophenol [47]. The usual peak of parathion methyl is in the range of 250–270 nm, which supports our findings. The interaction of parathion methyl with the C-dots solution results in the production of the other two peaks at 305 nm and 363 nm, followed by degradation and the development of a new interacted structure with enzyme and substrate [49].

Fluorescence spectroscopy showed the maximum emission peak of C-dots solution (Fig. 8a), on addition of Fe3+ the fluorescence intensity was quenched by 8 % which indicated the ‘turn-off’ concept of fluorescence property. As the MP-OP (1 ppm) was introduced it regained the fluorescence intensity by 3 % satisfying the concept of ‘turn-on’ fluorescence (Fig. 8a). The same can be visualized by placing the samples under UV light (Fig. 6e).

FTIR curve also suggested the formation and deformation of different bonds with and without the addition of pesticide (Fig. 6d). The FTIR spectra of C-dots solution of BG showed several small peaks at the fingerprinting region, and confirmed the formation of phenyl structures with different functional groups which were missing in the spectra of C-dots solution of BG + MP. The C-dots solution of BG + MP spectra as compared to the C-dots solution of BG spectra showed different chain opening structures, which indicated the conjugation of OPs with C-dots and enzyme complexes. More conjugation and deformation of bonds could be seen with increasing levels of added pesticide along with enzyme concentration. This supports the finding of the fluorescence spectroscopy in our study. The prepared probe solution was a mixture of Fe, which quenched the fluorescence and when the OP mixed with AChE along with ACh and ChOx was added, they interacted with the C-dots solution and formed a covalent bond which again enhanced the FL of the solution [43].

Metal ions like Fe3+ quenched the fluorescence and there was a coordination interaction between Fe3+ and oxygen atoms available on the surface of C-dots [21]. Parathion methyl interaction is a complex process, that is generally comprised of numerous steps including different preparation of solvents and media, different sources of reactive C-dots solution, reaction time, intra and inter-particle diffusion, mass transfer in the bulk liquid, and chemical reactions among the different compounds of the reaction vessel [50]. Also, addition of conjugated compounds along with enzyme-substrate complexes helps in the binding and well-established reactions of the same, which can be utilized as an “on-off-on” sensor for the detection of pesticides by utilizing low-cost household green raw waste. Here the neurotransmitter acetylcholine was hydrolysed to choline by AChE ( Equation (6) ) and then when it interacted with the choline oxidase (ChOx) enzyme, then choline was oxidized, and hydrogen peroxide generated (H2O2) ( Equation (7) ) which quenched the fluorescence intensity of C-dots [18]. When parathion methyl pesticide was interacted with the probe solution, the pesticide inhibited the activity of ChOx enzyme and subsequently increased the fluorescence intensity of the C-dots [18].
(6)
A c e t y l c h o l i n e +
H 2
O
→
A C h E
C h o l i n e + A c e t a t e
(7)
C h o l i n e +
O 2
→
C h O x
b e t a i n e
a l d e h y d e +
H 2
O 2

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity, calibration, LOD, and LOQ of MP-OP
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 8b-f, the fluorescence recovery could not be possible with these five pesticides, which were quenched by the C-Dots probe solution. Hence, the particular C-Dots solution was found to be practically applicable for the detection of MP-OP (Fig. 8a).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Turn-on’ fluorescence of C-dots in real sample analysis
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the concept of a ‘Turn-on’ fluorescence sensor in real samples of cabbage, apple and well water, the samples were spiked with different concentrations of Parathion Methyl OP as discussed above (Fig. 9 a). The samples were analysed by FL spectroscopy with excitation at 410 nm. All four samples spiked with the pesticides showed ‘Turn-on’ behaviour. Fig. 9 (b, c, d). With the increase in the concentration of OPs, the intensities increase gradually (Fig. 9- b, c, d), i.e., again it recovered the fluorescence behaviour which was masked by the addition of iron compound (the C-dots probe solution). These emphasized that this ‘Turn-on’ mechanism could be utilized for visual determination as a qualitative tool for the detection of OPs in a wide range of foods like vegetables, fruits and water.

From the study it was observed that, easily accessible household waste materials like peels of lemon, culinary bananas, bottle gourd, and sugarcane bagasse like waste can be the excellent source for the synthesis of functional C-dots. In the study all four-samples had green fluorescence and excitation dependent emission properties. Among the four C-dots obtained from the peel of BG showed emission at larger wavelength, sharp UV–Vis absorption peak, good quantum yield, and narrower size and evenly distributed with good amorphous structure. The photoluminescence behaviour of synthesized C-dots was seen to be dependent on carbonization process, the reaction environment like concentration, solvent used, the concentration of C-dots solution, the excitation wavelength, and the initial composition of raw materials. Presence of crude nitrogen in the raw material was found to be effective to improve quantum yield of the C-dots without surface passivation or doping. Also, due to the water holding capacity of sugarcane bagasse, the power and time level of synthesis was not effective for proper carbonization. The present studies support the use of low-cost household green waste-peel of BG for detection purpose of the parathion methyl organophosphate pesticide in real samples. The present study supported the ‘turn off’ fluorescence concept by interaction with specific metal ion Fe3+, which can be again recovered specifically with MP-OP pesticide. The same was proven to be same in real samples like water and fruits and vegetables for the detection of MP-OP. Present study will help in utilizing the same for the development of biosensors, optical probes, kits, films etc. in the detection field of pesticide. Immobilization of C-dots and quantum dots nanoparticles onto solid surfaces needs sound research in this area along with the development of the probe in the solid surface.
There is no conflict of interest to declare for the present submission.

Manisha Medhi: Writing – original draft, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Monica Yumnam: Methodology, Data curation. Pronab Mudoi: Visualization, Methodology. Poonam Mishra: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The author is thankful to MoFPI, UGC-SAP, DST-FIST, Dr. Bibekananda De, Prof Swapan K. Dolui, Priyankamoni Saikia, Sankur Phukan from Chemical Science Department, Tezpur University, Prof Pabitra Nath, Dr. Nabadeep Chamuah, Sritam Biswas from Physics department, Tezpur University, Parishmita, Sangeeta, Subhamoy, Krishna, Bhaskar and Pankhi from FET department, Tezpur University for providing the help to complete the research work.
